description: Module + topic outlines for the Java pro-track. Drives generateProTopic.mjs. Each topic's `concepts` list is the brief the generator gets — keep them concrete and assessment-friendly (a thing to teach AND to test).

m2_variables.name: Variables & Data Types
m2_variables.summary: How Java stores data. Primitive types, strings, operators, input, type casting. Foundation for every program that does anything other than print Hello World.

topics.name: Variables & Primitive Types
concepts: variable declaration syntax: <type> <name> = <value>;
the 8 primitive types: byte, short, int, long, float, double, boolean, char
default values and the difference between declaration vs initialization
naming conventions: camelCase, descriptive names, no reserved keywords
int vs long: when to choose each (memory + range tradeoff)
float vs double: precision tradeoff, why double is default in Java
primitives store values, not references — copy-by-value behaviour
reading variables back: System.out.println with concatenation

topics.name: Strings: Java's Special Type
concepts: String is an OBJECT not a primitive — capital S is the giveaway
string literals: "text in double quotes"
concatenation with + operator (works with any type on the other side)
string immutability — every operation returns a NEW string
common methods: length(), toUpperCase(), toLowerCase(), trim(), substring()
comparing strings: == vs .equals() — the most common Java beginner bug
escape characters: \n, \t, \", \\

topics.name: Arithmetic Operators & Type Promotion
concepts: the 5 arithmetic operators: +, -, *, /, %
integer division: 7 / 2 == 3 (not 3.5) — discards the remainder
modulo % for remainders: 7 % 2 == 1; common use cases (even/odd, wraparound)
type promotion: int + double → double automatically
operator precedence: * and / and % bind tighter than + and -
compound assignment operators: +=, -=, *=, /=, %=
increment/decrement: i++ vs ++i (pre vs post)
integer overflow: Integer.MAX_VALUE + 1 wraps to a negative number

topics.name: Reading User Input with Scanner
concepts: import java.util.Scanner — the import statement and why it exists
creating a Scanner: Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);
reading strings: sc.nextLine() vs sc.next() (one returns whole line, one returns one token)
reading numbers: sc.nextInt(), sc.nextDouble()
the newline-consumption gotcha: nextInt() leaves \n in the buffer, nextLine() then returns empty
always sc.close() (or use try-with-resources) to release System.in
input prompts: print() the prompt without a newline so the cursor sits next to it
